accepted.question_text: What is the maximum integer N for which there exists a packing array PA(N,14,9), meaning an N x 14 array with entries from {0,1,...,8} such that every 2-column subarray contains each ordered pair of symbols at most once?

evidence.quote: A "Packing Array" PA(N,k,v) is an N x k array (N rows and k columns), with each entry from the v-set {0,1,...v-1}, so that every N x 2 subarray contains every ordered pair of symbols at most once. Given (N,k,v), we want to construct PA(N,k,v).
The upper bound is N <= 21 [Colbourn and Dinitz, 2006], so it is an open question whether N = 21 is possible.
It succeeds in constructing a Packing Array with N = 21 for k = 14 v = 9, fully resolving this open question.

accepted.question_text: Does there exist a skew weighing matrix W(18,9), meaning an 18 x 18 matrix with entries in {0,1,-1} such that W W^T = 9I, each row and column has exactly 9 nonzero entries, distinct rows are orthogonal, and the matrix is skew-symmetric (W^T = -W)?

evidence.quote: A "weighing matrix" W(n,w) with parameters (n,w) is an n by n square matrix (n rows and n columns) with entries in {0,1,-1} that satisfies W W^T = wI. That is, W times its transpose is equal to the constant w times the identity matrix I. The weighing matrix will have w nonzero entries in each row and each column. And each pair of distinct rows is orthogonal (dot product zero). Given (n,w), we want to construct "SkewW", a skew weighing matrix W(n,w) that satisfies these properties and is also a skew matrix: that is, W^T = -W.

Table 1: Open Instance Results. For each combinatorial design problem, the smallest open instances from the Handbook are listed, along with progress on these published elsewhere in the literature. The protocol CPro1 in Fig. 1 used the given definition as input to generate code, and this code then made further progress on the open instances, as indicated in the last column.

accepted.question_text: Does there exist a balanced ternary design BTD(17,17;8,2,12;12,8), meaning an arrangement of 17 elements into 17 blocks of cardinality 12 that can be represented by a 17 x 17 incidence matrix with entries in {0,1,2}, where each element appears with total multiplicity R = 12, appearing with multiplicity 1 in exactly 8 blocks and multiplicity 2 in exactly 2 blocks, and every pair of distinct elements appears together exactly 8 times?

evidence.quote: A "Balanced Ternary Design" BTD(V,B;p1,p2,R;K,L) is an arrangement of V elements into B multisets, or blocks, each of cardinality K (K<=V) satisfying: 1. Each element appears R=p1 + 2*p2 times altogether, with multiplicity one in exactly p1 blocks and multiplicity two in exactly p2 blocks. 2. Every pair of distinct elements appears L times. The BTD is represented by a V by B incidence matrix with elements in {0,1,2}. Given (V,B,p1,p2,R,K,L) we want to find BTD(V,B;p1,p2,R;K,L).

Table 1: Open Instance Results. For each combinatorial design problem, the smallest open instances from the Handbook are listed, along with progress on these published elsewhere in the literature. The protocol CPro1 in Fig. 1 used the given definition as input to generate code, and this code then made further progress on the open instances, as indicated in the last column.

accepted.question_text: Does there exist a balanced ternary design BTD(12,26;3,5,13;6,5), meaning an arrangement of 12 elements into 26 blocks of cardinality 6 that can be represented by a 12 x 26 incidence matrix with entries in {0,1,2}, where each element appears with total multiplicity R = 13, appearing with multiplicity 1 in exactly 3 blocks and multiplicity 2 in exactly 5 blocks, and every pair of distinct elements appears together exactly 5 times?

evidence.quote: A "Balanced Ternary Design" BTD(V,B;p1,p2,R;K,L) is an arrangement of V elements into B multisets, or blocks, each of cardinality K (K<=V) satisfying: 1. Each element appears R=p1 + 2*p2 times altogether, with multiplicity one in exactly p1 blocks and multiplicity two in exactly p2 blocks. 2. Every pair of distinct elements appears L times. The BTD is represented by a V by B incidence matrix with elements in {0,1,2}. Given (V,B,p1,p2,R,K,L) we want to find BTD(V,B;p1,p2,R;K,L).

Table 1: Open Instance Results. For each combinatorial design problem, the smallest open instances from the Handbook are listed, along with progress on these published elsewhere in the literature. accepted.question_text: Does there exist a symmetric weighing matrix W(21,9), meaning an 21 x 21 matrix with entries in {0,1,-1} such that W W^T = 9I, each row and column has exactly 9 nonzero entries, distinct rows are orthogonal, and the matrix is symmetric?

evidence.quote: A "weighing matrix" W(n,w) with parameters (n,w) is an n by n square matrix (n rows and n columns) with entries in {0,1,-1} that satisfies W W^T = wI. That is, W times its transpose is equal to the constant w times the identity matrix I. The weighing matrix will have w nonzero entries in each row and each column. And each pair of distinct rows is orthogonal (dot product zero). Given (n,w), we want to construct "SymmW", a symmetric weighing matrix W(n,w) that satisfies these properties and is also a symmetric matrix.
Table 2: Open Instance Results - Prototyping Set. This has the same format as Table 1, and the results in the last column were generated by the automated protocol CPro1.

accepted.question_text: What is the maximum integer m for which there exists an equidistant permutation array with parameters (12,8,m), meaning an m x 12 matrix whose rows are permutations of 0 through 11 and such that every pair of distinct rows differs in exactly 8 positions?

evidence.quote: An "equidistant permutation array" (EPA) with parameters (n,d,m) can be represented as an m by n matrix (m rows and n columns), where each row is the permutation of the numbers 0 to n-1. Each pair of distinct rows must differ in exactly d positions. Given (n,d,m), we want to construct an equidistant permutation array (EPA) with these parameters.
Table 2: Open Instance Results - Prototyping Set. This has the same format as Table 1, and the results in the last column were generated by the automated protocol CPro1.
